Tornado watch issued for Calgary; severe weather reported throughout southern, central Alberta   Environment Canada has issued both a tornado watch and a severe thunderstorm warning for the city of Calgary.

Council eyes expansion of program that gets kids walking to school   The Active and Safe Routes to School Program has made improvements to infrastructure near 24 Calgary schools aimed at making it easier for children to walk to school

Risk assessment on convicted killer has finally begun, lawyer tells court   Confessed killer Megan Elizabeth Springstead has finally reached the front of the line at the Southern Alberta Forensic Psychiatry Centre, court was told Thursday.
Suspect now facing manslaughter charge in Forest Lawn shooting that turned deadly   Around 8:10 a.m. on June 14, police were called for reports of a shooting in the 2200 block of 45 St. S.E.

'Recognized and heard': Volunteers hope papal visit can be next step towards reconciliation   Volunteers working to prepare sites ahead of the arrival of Pope Francis later this month say they hope the pontiff’s visit will be the next step towards reconciliation for Alberta’s First Nations.
Heritage Park's S.S. Moyie expected back on the water this weekend after mechanical issues   The S.S. Moyie had been out of service since July 1

Belly on up: Your city-wide guide to Stampede breakfasts

Telus Sky officially opens its doors to Calgary   The building brings 750,000 square feet of Class AA office space and retail to the sector, as well as 326 residential units
Downtown office vacancy rate hits new high of 33.7 per cent   The vacancy rate is by far the highest in Canada, which has a national rate of 16.9 per cent in downtown cores
'Massive wealth' Canadians stockpiled during pandemic being chipped away, study says   Amount lost will depend on whether Bank of Canada can stick a 'soft landing' for economy
Canada's trade surplus just blew away expectations   Highest since 2008 on surging oil prices

Germany pleads with Canada to release Russian turbine critical for gas flows   The turbine, built in Canada by Siemens Energy AG, was sent to Montreal for repairs but became stranded due to sanctions on Russia
Melissa Mbarki: Indigenous Canadians need a federal leader like Pierre Poilievre   We need a federal political leader who pursues transparency and questions how money is spent

John Ivison: In a battle over integrity, Patrick Brown is badly outmatched   If you were to poll knowledgeable Conservatives on who they trusted more — Brown or organizing committee chair Ian Brodie — there would be one very clear winner
Braid: Leadership hopefuls for two parties are pouring in for Stampede   In provincial and federal ridings all over the city, most MLAs and MPs will be holding their own Stampede events
Your letters for July 6, 2022   Underfunding at the root of education issues Re: Teacher exits threaten class sizes; Early retirement, stress leaves rise amid forecast of higher enrolment, July 4

Opinion: It was a privilege to have been ‘Robbed' by late finance pioneer Rob Peters   It was late spring 1996 when I first met Rob Peters. He was the affable and immensely energetic pioneer of Calgary’s then fledgling finance industry. His call came at 11 a.m., a full 25 years after he founded Peters & Co.
True Crime Byline: We should have seen JoAnn Wilson's murder coming   Leader-Post city editor Barb Pacholik recalls the many turns of the Colin Thatcher case across decades.

True Crime Byline: How one childhood incident pushed Dawn Crey into a downward spiral from which she would never escape   The new podcast True Crime Byline looks back at The Vancouver Sun investigation into Vancouver's missing women, a case that eventually led to the arrest and conviction of Robert Pickton. This profile of missing woman Dawn Crey was part of a series of investigative stories published in 2001.

True Crime Byline: A Politician Murdered his Ex Wife. We Should Have Seen it Coming   Looking back on the murder of JoAnn Wilson today, what's most surprising is that we were surprised it happened at all.

True Crime Byline podcast: The Article that Blew Up a Murder Trial   Listen to this podcast episode for the shocking story of how a National Post article about a teen vampire led to a mistrial, as told by reporter Joseph Brean

Douglas Todd: Canadian cops far less likely to kill, or be killed, than U.S. counterparts   Analysis: Canadian police are often accused of using excessive force, but a U.S. officer is five times more likely than a Canadian cop to shoot and kill.

Actor James Caan — known for The Godfather, Misery and Elf — dies at 82   'Not only was he one of the best actors our business has ever seen, he was funny, loyal, caring and beloved,' a friend of the actor said

At the Oasis: Michael Perks wants his art to spark conversations at the Calgary Stampede   Two years ago, Michael Perks came across a hulking piece of farm machinery on his uncle’s land outside of Nanton.
Preview: History meets hip hop as Hamilton finally makes its way to Alberta   The wait is over.

Community comes together to ensure Passion Play resumes in Drumheller Badlands   After a forced two-year hiatus, The Badlands Passion Play on the outskirts of Drumheller is back celebrating its 27th season.
Canadian movie depicting Hindu goddess smoking sparks outrage in India   'Hurting Hindu sentiments cannot be allowed to go unpunished,' warned one social-media commenter
Treliving’s priority remains unchanged — signing Gaudreau and Tkachuk to long-term pacts   Come Friday, there will be cowboys on bulls and midway-goers on rollercoasters at the Calgary Stampede.
Rising bull rider Blake Smith arrives at Cody Snyder Bullbustin’ event on a mission   Blake Smith has burst onto the Professional Bull Riders Canada scene in a big way this season.

Former defenceman Bryan Marchment dies at 53 while in Montreal for NHL draft   Marchment played parts of 17 NHL seasons from 1989-2006 with Winnipeg, Chicago, Hartford, Edmonton, Tampa Bay, San Jose, Colorado, Toronto and Calgary
STAMPS NOTES: Faster start a priority   EDMONTON — The Calgary Stampeders are fully aware of their own bad habit of starting games slowly this season.
Like the 'West of old,' Stamps look to keep pace in competitive division   EDMONTON — There’s no room for error for the Calgary Stampeders.

And They're Off: From the Archives - The Stampede in the 1920s   The Calgary Stampede has long been a part of this city’s history. In recognition of this year’s event, on 10 weekdays (between July 4 and July 15), we’re sharing archival photo highlights and and events of note from the first 10 decades of the Greatest Outdoor Show on Earth. This instalment: The Stampede in the 1920s.

Chorney-Booth: Pursuit celebrates mountain cuisine with two new restaurants in the Rockies   Banff has perhaps been a bit slower than Calgary in its adoption of an “eat local” philosophy, but over the last few years many chain restaurants on the main drag have given way to more regional fare, and longer-running independent restaurants are moving to create memorable food that evokes a taste of the natural surroundings. Along with the growing juggernaut that is the Banff Hospitality Collective and the collection of restaurants at the Banff Springs Hotel, Pursuit, the global tourism company that grew out of Brewsters, has been leading the charge when it comes to developing a distinctive Rocky Mountain cuisine.

Calgary Horticultural Society: Nasturtiums are a multi-purpose beauty   I fell in love with jewel-coloured nasturtiums after someone dared me to eat one of the flowers. Very dubiously, I picked a lovely, brilliant red one, took a tiny bite and experienced the terrific peppery taste of it. The rest of it followed that first nibble, and I have been munching happily on nasturtium flowers and leaves and pickling their green seeds as capers ever since.

How We Live: The city abounds with people magnets   In our wistfully short summers, I crave being outside. And when the weather gods smile on us, I can’t get enough of walking, cycling, people-watching and dining outdoors in our lovely city.
Oleksyn: Toast Canada's birthday with some homegrown wines this weekend   Canada is a big country with a growing thirst for wine.
Volkswagen shows off the Phaeton successor it never built   The handsome top-line sedan was killed off as the automaker turned to electrification instead
This Stellantis designer wants to remove all screens from car interiors   Because a black screen covered in fingerprints isn’t very “sexy,” is it?
Most American drivers interested in shopping EVs: study   And unfamiliarity with them seems to be a major factor for those who aren't, says the Consumer Reports survey
36-year-old driver hauled from flaming wreckage on the QEW   Bystanders leap into action while car burns
VW introduces new Amarok pickup built with Ford bones   And, no, you still can’t have this Ranger-based truck in Canada—at least not yet anyway
TikTok Tuesday: #healthycoke — a trend that’s not so healthy for your teeth (WATCH)   It may be low in sugar, but carbonated water mixed with vinegar is a terrible choice for your teeth.
Putting our phones on silent mode only makes them more tempting, study says   Blame FOMO for how hard it can be for us to stop looking at our phones all the time.

Justin Bieber 'did something very important for the facial difference community'   Advocate Alim Somji says the pop star challenges the typical images of facial difference portrayed in the media as villains, such as Scar from the movie The Lion King.

WATCH: We take our medications out of the bathroom thanks to this TikTok trend   A bathrooms isn't a great storage option for meds, as the swings in temperatures and moist conditions can cause them to lose potency.
What are the signs of pancreatitis?   Acute and chronic pancreatitis are typically diagnosed between the ages of 40 and 70, with men more likely to be diagnosed across all age groups.

Joe Rogan responds to border drug seizure: ‘You should take all these drugs and rethink your life’   Border agents seized small amounts of ecstasy, cannabis and psilocybin mushrooms from a vehicle entering New York.
One of Canada's biggest universities will have a pot store on campus   The planned store would represent the pot company Burb's eighth and final one allowed in the province under its retail cap.
Ontario casinos laundered $3 million in illegal cannabis proceeds: RCMP   Weed worth approx. $24.8 million confiscated.

Man's ploy to flush over 20 kilograms of cannabis down the toilet leads him straight to jail   Officers also found 21 kilograms of weed, additional bags of prepackaged marijuana, a handgun and over $16K in cash.
Clint Eastwood handed $2.6 million after winning a second trademark infringement CBD lawsuit   Company did not have permission to use his image or likeness.
